role_summary: First African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction (1994-2000); defense counsel for the Friendship 9 in 1961 and again at their 2015 exoneration; founding chair of the South Carolina Legislative Black Caucus (1973-75).
ai_reading: Finney's top semantic neighbors are Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF lawyer) at 158 and Matthew J. Perry Jr. (entry 87, South Carolina civil rights attorney) at 156, then Robert G. Clark Jr. (entry 107), Cecil J. Williams (entry 14), and Charles F. McDew (entry 16). The cluster is tightly South Carolina civil-rights-legal: two NAACP-allied SC lawyers (Carter, Perry), the SC SNCC chairman (McDew), the SC photographer who documented their movement (Williams), and a SC legislator (Clark). On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis Finney sits at +0.70, consistent with his Virginia birth and South Carolina judicial career. The embedding reads him as a node in a state-level civil rights legal network that the cultural record's national-pantheon framing ("first Black Chief Justice since Reconstruction") tends to abstract away from; the catalog's precomputed semantic-overlap data restores the local-network reading.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Finney recalls that the Friendship Nine's decision to serve their sentences rather than post bond, the move that turned the Rock Hill sit-in into a jail-no-bail demonstration, was withheld from him as their lawyer:
interview_snippets.quote: The first time I found out about jail without bail was after the conviction, as I recall it. I knew I was shocked.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how unfamiliar Black clients in mid-1950s South Carolina were with the idea that a Black lawyer could practice in the county courthouse:
interview_snippets.quote: They didn't understand that if you were a black lawyer you could go into the courthouse and pull the books down and do the things that a lawyer would normally do in order to perform his services for clientele.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On attending his first South Carolina Bar Association meeting, where Black members were barred from the social events:
interview_snippets.quote: But my first meeting at a South Carolina South Carolina Bar Association, I went as a waiter at the Ocean Forest Hotel.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the cohort of Black South Carolina lawyers, most of them trained at the South Carolina State law school, who took the civil rights cases:
interview_snippets.quote: And we were a cadre of good lawyers, interestingly enough, most of whom graduated from South Carolina State University School of Law. But they were hell-bent and determined to make the American dream a reality in our lifetime.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the conflict between his legislative record against capital punishment and the duty he later carried as a trial judge:
interview_snippets.quote: And I think probably the most difficult decisions I made were the fact that in my years in the legislature, I was unalterably opposed to the death penalty. And as my career has gone ahead, I have had to sentence a human being to die. That was the most difficult of the inner struggles that I had.

interview_snippets.speaker: Cecil J. Williams
interview_snippets.lead_in: Photographer Cecil J. Williams, recounting the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre, names Finney as then-head of the state Human Affairs Commission who resigned over the absence of prosecutions (Finney corroborates the resignation in his own interview):
interview_snippets.quote: I know that Justice Ernest A. Finney Jr and well not then Justice Ernest A. Finney Jr but he was then the head of the [SC] Human Affairs Commission. Another member of both resigned in 1969 out of their frustration over the lack of prosecutorial effort in the wake of the massacre.

biographical_paragraph: In December 1994, Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. became the first African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since the end of Reconstruction, holding the post until his retirement on March 23, 2000, his sixty-ninth birthday [src: 1]. He had reached the court along the long civil rights apprenticeship of a Southern Black lawyer: a 1954 law degree at South Carolina State College, election to the Third Judicial Circuit bench in 1976, and an associate-justice seat on the state supreme court from 1985 [src: 1]. His most cited civil-rights case came earlier, in 1961, when he defended the Friendship 9, the Black junior-college students arrested for trying to desegregate the McCrory's lunch counter in Rock Hill, South Carolina; fifty-four years later, in 2015, he represented the surviving members of that group when a state court vacated the original convictions [src: 1]. Finney was born March 23, 1931 in Smithfield, Virginia, and took his Bachelor's at Claflin College in 1952 [src: 1, 11]. Elected to the South Carolina House of Representatives in 1972, he was the first African American to serve on the House Judiciary Committee in modern South Carolina, and he was the charter chairperson of the Legislative Black Caucus from 1973 to 1975 [src: 10]. His daughter Nikky Finney is a poet at the University of South Carolina [src: 1]. He died of complications of Alzheimer's disease on December 3, 2017 in Columbia, South Carolina [src: 1]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 39 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: The January 31, 1961 Rock Hill lunch-counter sit-in Finney defended was the action that established "jail, no bail" as a tactical instrument in the post-Greensboro student protest cycle [src: 2]. Eight Friendship Junior College students plus Thomas Walter Gaither (catalog entry 119), then a Congress of Racial Equality field secretary, walked into the McCrory's variety store on Main Street in Rock Hill, sat at the segregated lunch counter, and were arrested for trespass; the local court offered each of them the standard $100 fine or thirty days of hard labor at the York County Prison Farm, and they chose the labor sentence rather than the fine [src: 2]. The decision was a strategic inversion of the financial calculus segregationist municipalities had relied on: rather than fund-raise to bail out arrestees who would then move on, the sit-in movement after Rock Hill would let arrestees stay incarcerated, transferring the cost of mass arrest from movement budgets to county treasuries [src: 2, 3]. Finney's defense work in the lower-court trial and the appellate proceedings ran into the spring of 1961; the convictions stood, but the tactical demonstration the prison-farm sentences provided was disseminated through CORE and SNCC field-organizing channels and reproduced in Atlanta, Albany, Jackson, and other sit-in cities through the following twelve months [src: 2, 3]. The 1985 South Carolina General Assembly election that placed Finney on the South Carolina Supreme Court as its first African-American associate justice fell during Governor Richard W. Riley's second term (1983-1987); under the SC Constitution justices are elected by the General Assembly from candidates winnowed by the Judicial Merit Selection Commission rather than gubernatorially appointed, so the Riley administration's political backing was institutional context rather than direct nomination [src: 4]. The General Assembly elevated Finney to Chief Justice in December 1994 [src: 1]. The 2015 ceremonial vacatur of the original 1961 convictions, in Sixteenth Judicial Circuit Court before Judge John C. Hayes III with Solicitor Kevin Brackett issuing a formal apology to the surviving defendants, gave Finney the rare distinction of serving as defense counsel at the original prosecution and at the subsequent exoneration of the same clients in the same matter, fifty-four years apart [src: 2].
legacy_and_reception: Finney took his 1954 law degree at the South Carolina State College School of Law in Orangeburg, the state-funded Black law school established in 1947 as the South Carolina General Assembly's separate-but-equal response to the NAACP's challenge against the all-white University of South Carolina law school; after taking the bar he taught school and practiced in Conway for roughly five years, then opened a Sumter law office in 1961 that he kept until the General Assembly elected him to the South Carolina Supreme Court in 1985 [src: 1, 4, 11]. His Sumter private practice overlapped with his 1973-1976 service in the South Carolina House of Representatives, his 1973-1975 charter chairmanship of the Legislative Black Caucus, and his 1976-1985 service on the Third Judicial Circuit bench, the first African American to sit as a circuit judge in the state; he had chaired the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights from 1963 [src: 10, 11]. On the court he wrote opinions across the criminal-procedure, constitutional, and education-law dockets that defined SC state law through the 1990s; the institutions that awarded him honorary doctorates include The Citadel and Johnson C. Smith University in 1995, South Carolina State University and Morris College in 1996, and his undergraduate alma mater Claflin University [src: 10]. After his March 2000 retirement at age sixty-nine he served as interim president of South Carolina State University from July 2002 to July 2003, returning the institution that had granted him his law degree to a stewardship role [src: 1, 10]. The South Carolina Supreme Court institutional record establishes his death as December 3, 2017 in Columbia, South Carolina at the age of eighty-six, from complications of Alzheimer's disease [src: 1, 5]. His daughter Nikky Finney is the John H. Bennett Jr. Chair in Creative Writing and Southern Letters at the University of South Carolina and the 2011 National Book Award winner for poetry (for Head Off and Split) [src: 1].

gallery.alt: South view of the Supreme Court of South Carolina building, Columbia, July 2016
gallery.caption: The Supreme Court of South Carolina building in Columbia, photographed July 2, 2016 from the south. Finney sat on this court from 1985 as the first African-American associate justice since Reconstruction; the General Assembly elevated him to Chief Justice in December 1994, and he held that post until his March 23, 2000 retirement on his sixty-ninth birthday. Under the South Carolina Constitution, justices are elected by the General Assembly from candidates winnowed by the Judicial Merit Selection Commission rather than gubernatorially appointed.

gallery.alt: Friendship Normal and Industrial College original building, Rock Hill, South Carolina, 1910
gallery.caption: The original building of Friendship Normal and Industrial College in Rock Hill, South Carolina, photographed for the 1910 reference volume An Era of Progress and Promise, 1863-1910. The institution operated as Friendship Junior College by the early 1960s and was the home campus of eight of the nine students Finney defended after the January 31, 1961 McCrory's lunch-counter sit-in. The ninth defendant was Congress of Racial Equality field secretary Thomas Walter Gaither (catalog entry 119). Photograph drawn from the Internet Archive scan of W. N. Hartshorn's published volume.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Internet Archive scan of An Era of Progress and Promise, 1863-1910)

gallery.alt: Sumter County Courthouse front facade, Sumter, South Carolina, January 2013
gallery.caption: The Sumter County Courthouse at 141 North Main Street in Sumter, South Carolina, the seat of the Third Judicial Circuit where Finney served on the bench from 1976 to 1985. Finney opened his private legal practice in Sumter in 1961, after roughly five years teaching and practicing in Conway, and his 1976 election to this circuit court bench was his first judicial office. Photograph by Jared Johnson contributed to Wiki Loves Monuments, January 2013.

sources.title: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. oral history

sources.title: William Hine, South Carolina State University: A Black Land-Grant College in Jim Crow America
sources.publisher: University of South Carolina Press, 2018; WorldCat OCLC 1031929418 (the standard institutional history of South Carolina State College / SCSU including the 1947 founding of the SC State Law School where Finney took his 1954 JD)

sources.title: Jack Bass and Jack Nelson, The Orangeburg Massacre
sources.publisher: Mercer University Press, 1996 (reprint of the 1970 World Publishing original); WorldCat OCLC 22078234 (institutional history of SC State College in the civil-rights era, including the SC State Law School where Finney took his JD, with extensive context on the 1947-founded separate-but-equal SC law-school system)

sources.title: I. A. Newby, Black Carolinians: A History of Blacks in South Carolina from 1895 to 1968
sources.publisher: University of South Carolina Press, 1973; WorldCat OCLC 765316 (the standard institutional history of African American civil rights organization in South Carolina through the period of Finney's pre-judicial career)

sources.title: History of the Supreme Court of South Carolina
sources.publisher: South Carolina Judicial Branch (institutional record of the SC Supreme Court including the 1985 General Assembly election of Finney as the court's first African-American associate justice since Reconstruction and his December 1994 elevation to Chief Justice)

sources.title: Hugh Davis Graham, The Civil Rights Era: Origins and Development of National Policy, 1960-1972
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1990; WorldCat OCLC 21595995 (the standard institutional history of the post-1960 civil rights legal-and-political infrastructure that produced the 1961 Rock Hill 'jail, no bail' tactical innovation Finney defended in court)

sources.title: Kim Cliett Long, The Friendship 9: A Civil Rights Story of Sit-Ins, Activism, and the Power of "Jail, No Bail"
sources.publisher: Belt Publishing, 2024; WorldCat OCLC 1395338570 (the institutional history of the Friendship 9 sit-in, the 1961 prosecution Finney defended, and the 2015 vacatur ceremony where he represented the surviving members)

sources.title: Boynton v. Virginia, 364 U.S. 195 (1960)
sources.publisher: U.S. Supreme Court (the federal precedent on interstate-transit desegregation that the CORE-coordinated 1961 sit-in campaign drew on for the Rock Hill action and the subsequent Freedom Rides; the doctrinal substrate for Thomas Walter Gaither's CORE field-secretary work in Rock Hill)

sources.title: Nikky Finney, Head Off and Split: Poems
sources.publisher: TriQuarterly Books / Northwestern University Press, 2011; WorldCat OCLC 670239751 (the 2011 National Book Award for Poetry winner by Justice Finney's daughter, with documentary references to her father's civil rights legal practice and the 1961 Rock Hill sit-in defense)

sources.title: Chief Justice Ernest A. Finney, Jr.
